questions.question: If f: A → B is a bijective function, then f⁻¹ exists and is:
questions.explanation: The inverse of a bijective (one-to-one and onto) function is also bijective.
questions.subject: Mathematics

questions.question: A function f: R → R defined by f(x) = x² is:
questions.explanation: f(x) = x² is not injective since f(1) = f(-1) = 1. It is not surjective over R since negative numbers have no preimage.
questions.subject: Mathematics

questions.question: The composition of two bijective functions is:
questions.explanation: If f and g are bijective, then gof is also bijective. The composition preserves both the injective and surjective properties.
questions.subject: Mathematics

questions.question: A relation R on set A is said to be an equivalence relation if it is:
questions.explanation: An equivalence relation must be reflexive (aRa), symmetric (aRb → bRa), and transitive (aRb, bRc → aRc).
questions.subject: Mathematics

questions.question: If n(A) = 3, the number of reflexive relations on A is:
questions.explanation: A reflexive relation must contain all 3 diagonal pairs. The remaining 3×3 - 3 = 6 pairs can be freely chosen: 2⁶ = 64. Wait - total 9 pairs, 3 fixed, 6 free: 2⁶ = 64. But answer should be 64 = 2^(n²-n) = 2^(9-3) = 2^6 = 64.
questions.subject: Mathematics

questions.question: Which of the following is a binary operation on the set of natural numbers?
questions.explanation: Addition is a binary operation on ℕ because a + b ∈ ℕ for all a, b ∈ ℕ. Subtraction and division may give results outside ℕ.
questions.subject: Mathematics
